retrieved_docs.title: Measles -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The following are differential diagnoses for measles: Drug eruptions Erythema Infectiosum Kawasaki disease Meningococcemia Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever Infectious Mononucleosis Parvovirus B19 infection Pediatric enteroviral infections Pediatric rubella Pediatric sepsis Pediatric toxic shock syndrome Scarlet fever Multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children Dengue Zika Syphilis

retrieved_docs.title: Dengue Fever -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The febrile phase: During the febrile phase, individuals typically experience a sudden onset of high-grade fever, reaching approximately 40 °C, which usually lasts for 2 to 7 days. Approximately 6% of cases may exhibit saddleback or biphasic fever, particularly in patients with DHF and severe dengue fever. The fever usually persists for at least 24 hours, followed by a subsequent spike lasting at least 1 more day. [10] Associated symptoms during this phase include facial flushing, skin erythema, myalgias, arthralgias, headache, sore throat, conjunctival injection, anorexia, nausea, and vomiting. Skin erythema manifests as a general blanchable macular rash within 1 to 2 days of fever onset and again on the last day. Alternatively, within 24 hours, a secondary maculopapular rash may develop.

retrieved_docs.title: Herpangina -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Herpangina has a clinical presentation similar to several childhood disorders. Table 1 gives a summary of the differences in the clinical presentations of these disorders. Other diseases and conditions that should be ruled out before making the diagnosis include: Eczema herpeticum Toxic shock syndrome Measles Varicella Kawasaki disease Insect bites Rocky Mountain spotted fever Drug eruption Erythema multiforme major

retrieved_docs.title: Erythema Infectiosum -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The most common and classic presentation of erythema infectiosum is a mild febrile illness with a rash. Initial symptoms of infection can include fever, malaise, myalgias, diarrhea, vomiting, and headache. After initial viremia, the classic erythematous malar rash involving the cheeks with surrounding oral pallor develops. This rash does not appear early in the disease process. The lesion is classically described as a “slapped-cheek rash” and may be the only clinical diagnostic finding (see Image . Fifth Disease, Slapped-Cheek Rash). This facial rash lasts 4 to 5 days. By the time the facial rash develops, the patient usually feels well, and the viremia has resolved. This rash is thought to be immune-mediated (see Image . Fifth Disease, Reticular Facial Rash).

retrieved_docs.title: Rickettsial Infection -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Dengue Emergent management of malaria Group A streptococcal infection Kawasaki disease Leptospirosis Measles Meningococcal infection Pediatric rubella Pediatric syphilis Pediatric toxic shock syndrome Vasculitis and thrombophlebitis

retrieved_docs.title: Retinitis -- History and Physical -- Dengue Fever
retrieved_docs.content: Dengue fever can cause retinal vasculitis, yellow subretinal lesions, and foveolitis, or a round yellowish lesion at the fovea. [41] Cerebriform retinitis has been reported in association with parotid swelling. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Dengue Fever -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Expanded dengue virus syndrome refers to unusual or atypical manifestations seen in patients with involvement of various organs such as neurological, hepatic, and renal. This syndrome can be associated with profound shock. Neurological manifestations may include febrile seizures in young children, encephalitis, aseptic meningitis, and intracranial bleeding. Gastrointestinal involvement might present as hepatitis, liver failure, pancreatitis, or acalculous cholecystitis. In addition, this syndrome can manifest as myocarditis, pericarditis, acute respiratory distress syndrome, acute kidney injury, or hemolytic uremic syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Parvoviruses -- History and Physical -- Erythema Infectiosum
retrieved_docs.content: The prodrome of symptoms begins during viremia, where patients experience a headache, myalgias, and fever. The exanthem develops one to one and one-half weeks later. It is important to know that the patient is not contagious once the eruption occurs. At this time, the patient will have a “slapped cheek” appearance, described as erythema of the cheeks, sparing the central face. This finding is the most recognizable for this disease. Adults may not have the facial rash with this infection. Later, a maculopapular eruption occurs that looks “lacy” and reticular and favors the extremities but occurs on the trunk to a lesser extent. The eruption can last anywhere from one to three weeks. The rash can worsen when exposed to heat or sunlight. Arthritis of the small joints may occur, namely the hands, feet, and knees; however, this is more common in adults. It is especially common in women and is rare in children, where it occurs in only 10%. [5] [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Parvoviruses -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis of erythema infectiosum includes scarlet fever, enteroviral infection, rubella, Still disease, erysipelas of the face, contact dermatitis, Kawasaki disease, roseola infantum, measles, and drug eruption. The differential diagnosis of PPGSS includes meningococcal infection, erythema multiforme, Henoch-Schonlein purpura, and chemotherapy-related painful acral erythema.

retrieved_docs.title: Malar Rash -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of a malar rash is based on the causative condition.

retrieved_docs.title: MMR Vaccine -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: M-M-R II vaccine: Adverse events reported with M-M-R II vaccine include fever, headache, irritability, and panniculitis. Cardiovascular events such as vasculitis, and gastrointestinal events including nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, parotitis, and pancreatitis have been noted. Otologic effects include otitis media and sensorineural deafness. Ocular events include conjunctivitis, retinitis, and optic neuritis. Hematologic events include leukocytosis, thrombocytopenia, purpura, and lymphadenopathy. Immunologic reactions include anaphylaxis, anaphylactoid reactions, and angioedema. Musculoskeletal complaints include arthralgia and myalgia. Neurological events include encephalitis, encephalopathy, Guillain-Barré syndrome, subacute sclerosing panencephalitis, acute disseminated encephalomyelitis, transverse myelitis, polyneuropathy, seizures (both febrile and afebrile), ataxia, syncope, ocular palsies, and measles inclusion body encephalitis. Respiratory events include pneumonia, rhinitis, and pharyngitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Purtscher Retinopathy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Recent viral illness or acute febrile illness, or dengue fever [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Meningococcal Disease (Neisseria meningitidis Infection)  -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians examining adults with a maculopapular rash and fever should consider alternate etiologies, including infectious mononucleosis, West Nile virus, Zika virus, human immunodeficiency virus, Ebola virus, Rocky Mountain spotted fever, ehrlichiosis, and hypersensitivity drug reactions. [48] Children with a maculopapular rash and fever should be considered for a differential diagnosis that includes Kawasaki disease, measles, scarlet fever, rubella, parvovirus B19, roseola, Epstein-Barr virus, and hand, foot, and mouth disease. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Rubeola (Measles)(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Measles should be distinguished from similar presenting exanthemic diseases of childhood, autoimmune processes, and adverse drug reactions. Rubella causes a rash similar to measles with head-caudal distribution, mild respiratory symptoms, and the absence of conjunctivitis. Still, it is accompanied by the presence of adenopathies—which is characteristic of this disease. Roseola is characterized by an illness beginning with a high fever, which subsides after a few days, accompanied by the appearance of a rash in the central part of the body, without the presence of Koplik points. Mononucleosis is a febrile viral disease, a characteristic course with few symptoms during childhood, contrary to what happens in more advanced ages. Mononucleosis manifests itself by pharyngeal compromise, polyadenopathy, and hepatosplenomegaly, and the rash can have different forms of presentation. In Kawasaki disease, there is an ocular compromise with the presence of conjunctivitis without exudate, and the respiratory compromise is not part of this pathology. Group A Streptococcus (particularly scarlet fever) may present with a similar rash (a coarse, sandpaper-like, blanching, erythematous) to measles in association with pharyngitis. [5] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Zika Virus -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients with acute Zika virus infections are either asymptomatic (60% to 80%) or have only mild symptoms. For Zika disease due to a mosquito bite, the estimated incubation phase between bite and symptoms is two to 14 days. In symptomatic infections, the most common symptoms\signs include:  rash (90% or more), conjunctivitis (55% to 82%), fever (65% to 80%), and headache (45% to 80%). The rash is typically maculopapular, and the fever is often low grade, and short-lived. Other common symptoms and signs include arthralgia (65% to 70%, myalgia (48% to 65%), and retro-orbital pain (39% to 48%).  Less commonly seen are edema, vomiting, and abdominal pain.  In areas with dengue fever and chikungunya, which have many symptoms and signs in common, it may be difficult to diagnose the etiology of the illness correctly. Conjunctivitis and rash more commonly are seen in Zika virus infections than dengue fever and chikungunya.

retrieved_docs.title: Roseola Infantum -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Measles Rubella Scarlet fever Viral exanthem Exanthematous drug eruption

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2675
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting an overlap in terminology where both terms describe the observable manifestations and patient‑reported experiences associated with poorly characterized medical disorders. This conceptual equivalence highlights the importance of recognizing that “symptoms” and “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” often refer to the same clinical features, facilitating clearer communication in diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFECTIVES FOR SYSTEMIC USE is a broad therapeutic class that includes both ANTIVIRALS FOR SYSTEMIC USE and antimycotics for systemic use. These subcategories represent systemic agents specifically targeting viral infections and fungal infections, respectively, and together they constitute the systemic anti‑infective armamentarium used in patient care.

Recognizing malaise, fatigue, and the link between immunity disorders and globulin deficiency, together with the shared pharmacologic strategies and contraindications, supports an integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approach in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

Collectively, these inter‑related classifications demonstrate how the imidazole core generates a diverse pharmacological portfolio, guiding clinicians in recognizing structural analogues, potential cross‑reactivity, and alternative therapeutic options across topical, gynecological, systemic antibacterial, antiprotozoal and antiparasitic indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is clinically associated with nonspecific chest pain and with fatigue (التَّعَب). These findings indicate that patients with oral candidiasis may present not only with typical oral lesions but also with systemic or atypical symptoms such as unexplained chest discomfort and generalized tiredness, underscoring the importance of considering this fungal infection in the differential diagnosis when such nonspecific complaints arise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 627
retrieved_docs.content: Remdesivir may be used to treat عدوى فيروسية, offering therapeutic benefit for patients with viral infections, whereas ribavirin may be employed to prevent عدوى فيروسية, serving as a prophylactic antiviral option. Both agents are relevant in the clinical management of viral diseases, with remdesivir focusing on treatment and ribavirin on prevention.

Within this group of nonspecific clinical manifestations, the specific finding of الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse) is recognized as a child element, illustrating that severe cardiovascular failure can be one of the many possible presentations encompassed by ill‑defined symptom complexes. This relationship underscores the importance of viewing individual symptoms as part of a larger, sometimes vague, clinical picture when assessing patients with ambiguous or poorly characterized disorders.
